name: Semeai / Capturing Race
description:
Problems which deal with a liberty filling battle between groups lacking two eyes.
[diagram:sgf=(;AW[aa][ab][ac][cd][dd][da][db][dc]AB[ca][cb][cc][ad][bd])]

name: Amateur Game
description: Problems taken from real amateur games. These can often prove instructive as most of us are still amateurs, and the situations that arise in such games are relevant to our experience.

name: Corner based
description:
Corner based problems.

See also "Side based", "Floating", "Whole Board Positions" and "Giant".

name: Side based
description:
Side based problems.

See also "Corner based", "Floating", "Whole Board Positions" and "Giant".

name: Connect
description: Try to connect stones.

name: Ko / Ko Threats
description: Best way to play in a ko, or ways to play to maximize ko threats later.

name: Encyclopedia of Life and Death - Easy
description: Cho Chikun's encyclopedia of life and death part first elementary problems

name: Self made
description: Problems made by the goproblems users themselves, with no book or other players inspiration.

name: Cutting
description: Cut your opponent's stones.

name: Auto Generated
description: These problems were generated with an AI-assisted tool, extracted from real amateur go games where a big mistake was made.

name: Multiple Choice
description: The possible moves are listed for the problem solver as choices.

name: Lack of Liberties (Damezumari)
description: "Shortage of liberties means an acute lack of liberties. Since stones with no liberties are captured, the phrase is used in certain situations to mean that some stone or stones will be captured, or would be captured if a certain play is made or is not made. E. g., "White cannot play atari because of a shortage of liberties," or "Black must connect because of a shortage of liberties." When a stone or stones has only one liberty, we say that it is in atari, so shortage of liberties is used when they have two or more liberties. Also, in a race to capture, the losing side may of course be said to have a shortage of liberties, but because that is a given, it is not put in those terms, as a rule. Of course, a shortage of liberties may be an aspect of a race to capture." - senseis.xmp.net/?damezumari

name: 9*9 games
description: Here you can find a problem: find the sequence leading you to victory on 9*9 board.

name: Local Choice
description: Multiple choice problems dealing with local tactics and shape. Generated from amateur games using KataGo. The goal is to create a class of problems that represent a common weakness in players: making local tactical decisions that aren’t life and death or tesuji situations – they are just about getting the best local result possible in terms of points, sente, influence, weaknesses, etc.

name: Snapback
description: The classic tesuji.

name: Ladder
description: Otherwise known as [senseis:shicho], ladder problems can be interesting in many different ways. Some are difficult to read due to the configuration of the stones at their end. Some fantastical creations lead to ladders bouncing all over the go board.

name: Whole Board Positions
description:
Real game problems where the moves must be played according to the rest of the board.

See also "Corner based", "Side based", "Floating" and "Giant".

name: GuanZiPu
description:
This ancient chinese text contains 1454 challenging problems. It's know as KanZuFu in Japanese.

[senseis:GuanziPu]

name: Squeeze Tesuji
description: "The Squeeze tesuji is a play by which you squeeze the opponent's stones into an inefficient dumpling shape. Often a successful squeeze includes sacrificing one or more stones to make the opponent's shape bad first." - Senseis

name: Instruction Required
description: It is necessary to read some instruction before solving these problems: trying to solve the problem before reading the introduction will not work. These problems often say things like "if you think you can kill this group, play at A. otherwise play at B."

name: Basic Shape
description: A standard shape, usually found in a corner or side. These will often appear in games; sometimes even out of josekis, and it's important to know the solution beforehand.

name: Middle based
description:
Middle based problems.

See also "Side based", "Corner based", "Whole Board Positions" and "Giant".

name: Xuan Xuan Qijing
description: This classic Chinese text dates from the fourteenth century, originally published in 1347 by Yan Defu and Yan Tianzhang. The problems take fanciful and historical aspects, with themes relating to stories and events. For example, in the 'Mother of Seven Sons' problem, the seven sons refers to seven white stones at the core of the problem. Known in Japanese as 'Gen Gen Go Kyo.' See [senseis:XuanxuanQijing]

name: Under the Stones
description: Otherwise known as ishi-no-shita. Playing where a group of stones was already removed from the board. These tesujis are notoriously hard to see while solving problems, never mind playing real games.

name: Geta (net)
description: These formations capture opponent stones by surrounding them and stopping them breaking out.

name: Fun
description: Whimsical problems of the kind that you'd never see in a real game, but are fun to solve.

name: 9x9 corner fights
description: things that help for defending the corner in a 9x9

name: Life and Death
description: Life and Death

name: Life on 2nd Line
description: Often one has to find life with stones only on the second line.

name: Game based
description: Problems based on game positions

name: Irregular board
description: Problems on an unusually shaped board.

name: Symmetrical
description: At least one axis of symmetry.

name: 4x4 problems
description: You can't play on a 4x4 board, but there are some nice problems. Solving them, you always have to look after the "best result"; that means, a win by two points is better than a win by one, and even a loss by three is better than a loss by two (and therefore the solution). This is a bit unusual, of course, but since there is only a limited number of interesting positions, this is a natural way to increase this number. Please note also that all problems use japanes rules.

name: Giant
description:
Non-real game problems that encompasses an entire board, or at least the majority of it. Otherwise known as 'chinrou.'"

See also "Side based", "Middle based", "Corner based" and "Whole Board Positions".

name: Pro Game
description: A position from a professional game. The classical way to learn.

name: Go Seigen Tsumego
description: A collection of 142 problems created by Master Go Seigen 9p, and out of which problems No.1 to No.110 are solved and explained in detail by his student O Rissei 9p.

name: 0 What Group?
description:
If you are unsure to what group a problem belongs please add it here and the moderators will index it for you.

(The "0" in the name is only there to make sure the group is at the top of the list, so people can find it more easily.)

name: Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Tesuji Go Problems Volume 2
description:
Volume 2: ISBN: 7-8105-1566-7
Snapback and Shortage of liberties problems.

name: Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Life and Death Go Problems Book 6
Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Life and Death Go Problems Book 5

name: Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Life and Death Go Problems Book 1
description:
Lee Chang-ho's Selected Life and Death Go Problems (Yi Chang-ho Jingjiang Weiqi Sihuo) is a set of six Chinese, go problem books. Each of these volumes includes 123 life and death problems.
In volumes 1-3, the first 60-problems are White/Black to live; the last 63-problems are White/Black to kill.
In volumes 4-6, the first 52-problems are White/Black to live; the last 71-problems are White/Black to kill.
Each page has one problem with the solution on the back w/ accompanying Chinese text. The problems become more difficult as one proceeds through the set of six books. The second half, to kill problems, of each book seems to be noticeably harder than the first half, to live problems.

Volume 1: ISBN: 7-8105-1521-7

name: Keima Crush
description:
Throwing in on a group to stop an eye in a certain formation, where the throw-in prevents making an L shape, and after taking the throw-in the eye can be prevented with an outside atari.

name: Tesuji by James Davies (WIP)
description:
The problems from the book "Tesuji" by James Davies. It is part of the Elementary Go Series. This is not intended to be a replacement for the book, but rather an easier way to review the problems after you've read it.
-
The solutions are not from the book (due to copyright) and have been written by a variety of users. On occasion there were very similar problems already on the site so I added those instead. I tried to add corresponding page numbers to the problems I added in the "Sources" section so you can review those chapters when you miss one. 

- 

Not included: 
- Problems 6 on page 57 (Was very similar to problem 3)
- I just finished page 76 anything past that is not added. This is still a wip.

name: Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Tesuji Go Problems Volume 1
description:
Volume 1: ISBN: 7-8105-1565-9

name: Yi Ch'ang-ho's Selected Life and Death Go Problems Book 4
Golden Chicken Standing on One Leg
description: Killing a group on the side with some stones splitting in two halves. Neither side of the opponent can approach. One of the opponent sides may have an eye, but this tesuji still kills.

name: 7x7 Endgames
description: Endgames on a 7 by 7 board.

name: Shape
description: Try to make good shape for your stones or bad shape for your opponent's. One of the most important skills in go.

name: Counting Points
description: How many points is a move worth? These problems test your counting ability.

description: problems that are too hard for me currently, but I accidentally solved them on the site

name: Best move with letting enemy live
description: When one player well do killing enemy's group, then it's happy. But some cases are impossible to kill. But go is not representing only "winning games". Anytime doing the best is winning for self.

name: Double Snapback
description: An infamous tesuji where two groups are in snapback at the same time. A favorite of wildly improbable problems.

name: 1st Line Tesuji

Where all the basic, elementary and classical life and death to be included.

Generally those common life and death.

by xinwen

name: Strange
description: Problems, jokes, etc. whose solutions are not based on normal go play.

name: order
description: the same moves in both correct and incorrect sequences but the order in which they are played changes everything

name: L-Group
description:
Problems dealing with the basic 'L' shape in the corner (or derivatives of).

L* denotes the L group without the corner stone.

name: J-Group
description: Problems dealing with the basic 'J' shape in the corner (or derivatives of). See [senseis:jgroup]

name: Tesuji
description: General or non-specific tesuji

name: Stories
description: The problems have associated stories.

name: How Many Ways
description: These problems ask you not just to kill or live, but how many ways there are to do it. This is good training, because many go players quit as soon as they find one way, without examining all the possibilities that may have different effects on the surrounding situation.

name: Hideo Otake â€” Opening Theory Made Easy
Black or White
description: Problems where the first move consists of choosing to attack, if the user thinks that he can kill, or choosing to defend, if the user thinks that he cannot kill.

name: 5x5 Endgames
description: Endgame problems from 5x5 boards.

name: Timing
description:
The idea for a concept "Timing" came up when editing problem #3300.

Problems can be added into this group, when the question "when" to play a move or sequence is especially  important or the key issue. Rin Kaiho (Lin Haifeng): "Go is about the order of moves". 
Examples could be YOSU-MIRU moves (probe in a position to see how the partner responds).
It will include many examples with correct paths where the end-position is the same as in a wrong solution, but did evolve in another way. 
Other examples would be positions which are not played out completely, rather stopped at a certain point in order to wait for a better (the best) time to proceed (in ways which can be decided on better later). This is the 2nd aspect of YOSU-MIRU.

I would understand the context narrower than given in http://senseis.xmp.net/?Timing.

name: Notch Group
description: 2nd line groups with a "notch".

name: Dual Threat Tesuji
description: Tesuji that threaten 2 things and where the opponent can only defend against one.

name: Hamete
description: A hamete is a trick move. If you do not know the correct answer you run the risk of ending up with a very bad position. While if you do know, your opponent will mostly suffer only a small loss

name: Nakade

name: Escape
description: The concept of escaping with a group out of the local context, i.e. globally into the center (the contrary would be boshi) or on smaller scale, i.e. the other color cannot stop the escaper from collecting more liberties or confine him in any direct way.

name: Irregular Tesuji

name: Prevent the Connection

name: Carpenter's Square

name: Tripod

name: Monkey Jump
description: Problems in which this move is a likely contender for best play; either as territory reduction (endgame), or in a life and death way. It is not necessarily the solution.

name: Monkey Jump
description: Problems in which this move is a likely contender for best play; either as territory reduction (endgame), or in a life and death way. It is not necessarily the solution.

name: Shape Up! (5 kyu course)

These are the problems from the first two problems sets recommended for players around 5kyu, or weaker players reading through the book for their second time. https://cdn.online-go.com/shape_up.pdf

name: Shape Up! All Problem Sets
description:
Shape Up! is a public domain online go book on good shape written by Charles Matthews and Kim Seong-June.
I recommend you read it here https://cdn.online-go.com/shape_up.pdf
Then use this collection to practice what you've learned. These questions come from the book. Since Matthews and Seong-June were gracious enough to release this into the public domain, we are able to add their expert explainations in the problem tree.

name: 2nd line cornered
description: Life on the second line, bending around a corner

name: Damezamari on One Eye
description: These problems force a group to have one eye in damezamari -- then make it impossible to get the second eye through some tesuji on the other side.

name: 2-2 Crosscut
description: Problems where there's an obvious 2 - 2 cutting point to start the problem.

name: Corner Boxes
description: A perfectly rectangular box in the corner, which may or may not have open edges or enemy stones inside.

name: One Liberty Throw-In
description: This tesuji is for when you throw-in where it seems like a throw-in is silly. In this case, there is only one existing liberty affecting the group you're throwing in against. (Usually, there are two.) I realize this description makes little sense, but by looking at the problems in this group it should become more clear.

name: Large L-Group
description:
Problems dealing with the basic large 'L' shape in the corner (or derivatives of).
Large L* denotes the Large L-group without the corner stone.

name: Forcing Three on Edge
description: A tesuji where you force your opponent to extend his encroachment into your group to three stones along the edge. He can no longer let you catch two to take one back, and either dies in the corner or gives you another eye.

name: CGT
description: Combinatorial game theory is, among other things, about how to get the last point.  Sometimes these problems can be solved by standard endgame theory, sometimes not.  CGT is described on Sensei's Library.

name: 1035 tsumego part1 No.495

one problem but seperate to two problems in goproblems.com

name: assessment

name: Famous Problems
description: Special problems that stem from professional games or other specifically designed problems. For example: Shusaku's ear-reddening move and Dosaku's Nose hit.

name: Study Japanese sweet language!

When you visit Japan, please enjoy the time with the OMOTENASHI :)

name: Study Japanese sweet language!

This group contains of Japanese characters, Japanese language, and Japanese culture.
When you visit Japan, please enjoy the time with the OMOTENASHI :)

name: Diabolical Vol 1 - David Mitchell [SoumyaK4](Work In Progress)
description:
#### If you find wrong variations please let me know [here](https://soumyak4.in/#socials-n-stuff)

# Tsumego Info

Each problem contains multiple tsumego positions. In the book Black plays first but here it could be Wh1te. The player has the first move only, after that moves alternate as in a normal game.
The task is for Player to win in all positions. Sometimes Black's stones are under attack and need to live, sometimes Wh1te's stones should die. The result in every problem is clear, no ko fights do, or not do, there is no ko. Killing a dead group is just as bad as dying in gote.

# Credits

These problems are from "Diabolical" by David Mitchell a life and death training book. The book has 10 Warm Up, 40 Troublesome & 50 Diabolical training problems to help strong players(2Kyu+) improve their reading skills. There are NO answers in the book.

The book is published by the Australian Go Association. [You can purchase, donate and find a PDF](https://australiango.asn.au/aga-books)

# Shameless self-advert

### Check Out:
### [My Go Projects](https://soumyak4.in)
### [Weiqi Roadmap](https://weiqi.soumyak4.in)
### [BadukTube](https://baduktube.soumyak4.in)

name: The Peep n' Squeeze & Clamp n' Squeeze
description: The basic peep and squeeze is a peep on the first line and then an atari that forces your opponent for fill an eye. (See prob num 12073) Sometimes it doesn't literally have the peep though, some of these problems begin with something like a clamp or wedge but eventually transposes to the peep n squeeze shape.

name: Igo Hatsuyo-Ron

For what I think were political reasons, it fell out of print, but has recently been reprinted. It's filled with challenging and fascinating tesujis that look like they've been lifted from games, all organized by general theme.

description: By giving up four stones on the edge, you can throw back in and prevent any eye at all. This is usually useful when connecting out would end with some shortage of liberties along the edge.

name: gogalar rules
description:
There are many go rules Chinese rules, Japanese rules, Korean rules, American rules, AGA rules and New Zealand rules. I wanted to create my own rules.

1. Not to recapture and count ko even if there are ko threats.
2. Scoring are New Zealand rules included.
3. If one player passes with no intersections to play, if another player passes after making the territory unconditionally, the faster territory made player wins.
4. You can use Irregular boards.
